summary: Jess Frazelle demonstrates how to run desktop applications and Docker-in-Docker using Docker's new user namespace support coming in version 1.10. She walks through practical examples including Chrome, the notify-osd daemon with irssi IRC client integration, and explains how to achieve Docker-in-Docker without the --privileged flag by carefully selecting only the necessary capabilities (CAP_SYS_ADMIN and CAP_NET_ADMIN) and adjusting seccomp/apparmor profiles.
key_points: User namespace support moved from Docker Experimental to stable in version 1.10.0
Running Chrome in a container with user namespaces only requires adding --group-add video and --group-add audio
Desktop notification daemons can be containerized and shared with other containers via volume sharing
Docker-in-Docker without --privileged is possible by using only CAP_SYS_ADMIN and CAP_NET_ADMIN capabilities
The --privileged flag can be replaced with more granular security controls
money_quotes: Now I have always run my notifications daemon in a container, because that stuff is nasty to install, so many dependencies, ewwww.
What is --privileged actually doing? Well for starters it's allowing all capabilites, but… do we really need them all? The answer is no.. all we really need is CAP_SYS_ADMIN and CAP_NET_ADMIN.
Makes sense right.. we obviously want to be a member of those groups to watch Taylor Swift music videos.

When running the docker daemon with user namespace support, you cannot use docker run flags like --privileged, --net host, --pid host, etc. These are for pretty obvious reasons so I'm not going to get into it, if you want to know more RTFM.
key_insight: The --privileged flag is a sledgehammer when you usually only need a scalpel—understanding which specific Linux capabilities you actually need enables more secure container configurations.
